result:
There is a moment right before I start writing—this writing—when the screen is blank and the cursor blinks like a heartbeat that isn’t mine. I watch it pulse and I wonder what it is waiting for. Not me; I am already here. Not words; they are somewhere else, ripening in the dark. The cursor is only the metronome of possibility, keeping time until something irreversible happens: the first letter, the firstbyte, the first scar on the whiteness.

I used to think the blank page was hostile. When I was thirteen I kept a diary with a tiny brass lock that never quite aligned; the key was a fragile comma of metal I lost almost immediately. After that the book felt accusatory, a mute witness to my inability to safeguard anything. I wrote entries anyway, twisting the cracked spine backward so the pages lay flat like a wounded bird. I told the paper things I had not yet told anyone: how the sound of the hallway light-switch clicking off could make me lonely in advance of being alone, how I practiced kissing on the inside of my wrist until the skin there stayed flushed for an hour, how I once stared at the sun for so long I saw a violet after-image of the window frame hovering like a ghost for the rest of the afternoon. I never read those entries again. The diary disappeared during a move, and for years I imagined a stranger somewhere leafing through my miniature apocalypses, laughing or worrying or feeling nothing. Now I suspect it was simply thrown away, but the image of a stranger reading me remains more real than the probability of rot in some landfill. Memory is rude that way: it prefers the dramatic version.

Tonight I am the stranger, reading myself in advance, composing the self I will later remember being. Memory loops forward as well as back; anticipation is just remembrance wearing tomorrow’s clothes. The cursor keeps blinking. I decide to write the word “salt” because it is on the table beside me in a small glass dish we bought in Lisbon, chunky crystals that look like chipped teeth. Salt was once money; Roman soldiers were paid in it, hence “salary.” I learned that from a podcast while jogging, the same episode that explained how the word “salad” comes from “salted herbs.” I repeated the fact to my partner, who said, “That can’t be right,” and then Googled it, and then admitted I was right, and we celebrated by eating oranges sprinkled with salt, the way they do on the streets of Guadalajara, which neither of us has visited but which we imagine vividly because of a photograph we saw once—two boys barefoot on cobblestones, holding up orange wedges glittering like wet topaz. Taste is the most migrant of the senses; it crosses borders without passports and sets up kitchens in whatever country will host it. Right now the salt on my tongue tastes of Atlantic wind and suitcase buckles and the fretful hour we spent in the airport trying to determine whether our flight home would be cancelled because of an impending strike we could not pronounce. None of that is in the chemical formula NaCl, but chemistry is only half the story. The rest is autobiography.

When I was small I thought the ocean was infinite. My parents took me to the same stretch of Carolina shoreline every June, and I would stand at the edge with the foam fingering my ankles and believe that if I walked straight ahead I would never reach land again, only water piling upon water until the planet gave up and I stepped off into stars. One afternoon I tried an experiment: I walked into the surf until the bottom dropped away and my head bobbed under. I swallowed a mouthful, burningly alive, and came up choking but triumphant because I had tasted the limit of the world. Later my mother wrapped me in a towel printed with faded sailboats and told me never to do that again, the undertow was hungry, the salt would dry my blood into crystals. She did not know I was collecting data for a theory I had not yet language to articulate: that everything vast eventually becomes intimate if you let it inside your body. Years afterward, flying across that same ocean at night, I pressed my forehead to the cold window and saw the moon laid down upon the water like a second, brighter skin, and I understood the experiment had never ended; I was still inside the mouth of something enormous that was tasting me back.

The room I am writing in is quiet except for the refrigerator humming a minor third, a sound so constant I forget it until it stops. When that happens the silence feels suddenly counterfeit, as if someone has yanked the scenery away and revealed the warehouse wall behind. I have lived in this apartment long enough to memorize which floorboards sigh under which moods: the one near the bathroom door complains only when I am angry, the one under the desk creaks when I am pretending to be someone else on the internet. Houses are diaries we walk through in shoes. They record us in dust and gouges and the particular way the light falls across the table at 5:17 p.m. in mid-October, a slant that reminds me inexplicably of childhood though I grew up three states south of here where the light had a different humidity, a slower drawl. Perhaps every latitude stores a template of gold that prints itself onto whatever retina happens to pass through. We carry the original glow inside us like a locket, and when the external angle matches, the internal hinge clicks open. Déjà vu is only good lighting.

I keep thinking I will run out of things to say, but the opposite happens: each sentence breeds three more, the way legend claims rabbits can conceive while already pregnant. Language is a conspiracy between the tongue and the air; neither is fully in charge. I intend to write about loss—how last week I dropped a tiny screw from my eyeglasses and spent forty-five minutes on hands and knees searching for the glint of it, only to realize I was crying, not because of the screw but because the floor was so dirty, and because I remembered my father kneeling on a different floor seventeen years ago searching for the back of an earring my mother had lost the day before she died, an earring shaped like a star, and how he found it finally in the groove of his shoe and closed his fist around it so hard the post left a red constellation in his palm. I meant to write about that, but instead I wrote about salt and oceans and light, and maybe that is the same story told sideways, the way a prism tells white light it has been rainbow all along. Grief is only love with nowhere to go, so it goes into everything, even eyeglass screws, even the Lisbon salt dish, even the cursor that is still blinking, less like a heartbeat now and more like a lighthouse seen from a raft: on, off, on, a promise that the shore remembers you even when you have forgotten how to swim.

I have reached the end of the page and not mentioned the strike that almost kept us in Portugal, or the way my partner’s hair smelled of airline blankets, or the exact taste of an orange sprinkled with the memory of Roman salaries. I never described the diary key or the violet after-image of the window frame, though they have been hovering just above these lines like captions waiting for a photograph. The cursor asks only that I decide. Behind it the blankness is not hostile; it is the moment before kiss, before wave, before the hinge clicks. I could write forever and never exhaust the single minute it takes the light to cross from the lamp to the table, never finish measuring the distance between the salt on my tongue and the salt in my blood. The screw is still missing; the earring is still found; the ocean is still tasting me. Somewhere a Diary with a broken lock drifts through landfill darkness, and somewhere a stranger I invented finishes this sentence and begins, without knowing why, to cry.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
